text: The surgeon identifies the intraperitoneal appendix, with small bowel adhered to its lateral aspect. The base of the cecum is visible, and the surgeon gently separates the small bowel from the appendix to elevate it.

The surgeon identifies the intraperitoneal appendix with small bowel adhered to its lateral aspect. The base of the cecum is visible, and the surgeon carefully detaches the small bowel from the appendix to elevate it. The medial aspect of the appendiceal mesentery is exposed, and the surgeon lifts the lateral aspect of the appendix off the retroperitoneum, which has formed a phlegmon.

The surgeon frees the appendix from surrounding tissues, lifting it to expose the junction with the cecum. The medial aspect of the appendiceal mesentery and lateral aspect beneath the appendix are visible as the surgeon carefully separates the appendix from the retroperitoneum, which has formed a phlegmon.

The surgeon exposes the junction of the appendix to the cecum, revealing the inflamed area. The body of the appendix is freed from the phlegmon, and the remaining appendiceal mesentery is dissected. A potential iatrogenic perforation is visible near the base of the appendix. The junction with the cecum is cleaned to prepare for stapling, although an endo loop could also be used.

The surgeon continues to dissect the appendiceal mesentery, revealing a potential iatrogenic perforation near the base of the appendix. The junction with the cecum is exposed, and the remaining mesentery is cleared to create space for the stapler.

The surgeon inspects the staple line on the appendix for any defects or injuries, finding it intact. The appendix is placed into a retrieval bag and moved aside. The omentum is then brought down to cover the staple line for protection. The gallbladder appears secondarily inflamed but is not clinically significant.

The surgeon removes the appendix from the body, completing the appendectomy procedure.
